name: Participant Recruitment Channels
consequences: The choice of recruitment channels directly impacts the participant enrollment rate and the representativeness of the study population. Focusing on specialist referrals may yield higher-quality data but limit sample diversity, while broader outreach could increase enrollment but introduce more variability. The chosen channels also affect recruitment costs and the time required to reach target enrollment numbers.
options: Prioritize direct recruitment from the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic to ensure a consistent patient population
Expand recruitment to include national advertising campaigns targeting individuals with self-reported parasomnia symptoms
Partner with regional general practitioners to identify potential participants during routine check-ups
review: Controls recruitment volume vs. sample bias. Weakness: The options do not address the potential for selection bias based on socioeconomic factors.

name: Data Acquisition Tiering
consequences: The tiered data acquisition model balances comprehensive data collection with participant comfort and resource constraints. More frequent full PSG montages provide richer physiological data but can be burdensome for participants and costly to analyze. Conversely, relying solely on low-burden monitoring may miss subtle but important parasomnia events, reducing the study's sensitivity.
options: Increase the frequency of enhanced-night PSG to every other night for all participants to capture more events
Implement adaptive PSG scheduling based on real-time analysis of low-burden sensor data to trigger enhanced nights only when needed
Reduce enhanced-night PSG to only the first and last night, relying primarily on low-burden sensors for longitudinal data
review: Controls data richness vs. participant burden. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact of different sensor types on data quality and signal-to-noise ratio.

name: Data Sharing Scope
consequences: The extent of data sharing impacts the project's scientific impact and potential for collaboration, but also raises ethical and privacy concerns. Sharing only de-identified physiological data minimizes privacy risks but limits the utility of the dataset for researchers interested in behavioral or contextual factors. Including video data, even with de-identification, significantly increases privacy risks and requires more stringent data use agreements.
options: Share only summary statistics and aggregated data, protecting individual participant data entirely
Develop a secure, access-controlled platform for sharing fully de-identified physiological and video data with qualified researchers
Share de-identified physiological data and coded behavioral annotations, excluding video data entirely
review: Controls scientific impact vs. participant privacy. Weakness: The options do not address the potential for re-identification of participants through combined datasets.

name: Event Triage Model Development
consequences: The approach to event triage model development influences the efficiency and accuracy of parasomnia event detection. Focusing solely on agreement with human scorers may overlook novel event features or patterns detectable only through automated analysis. Prioritizing sensitivity may lead to a high false positive rate, while prioritizing specificity may miss genuine parasomnia events.
options: Develop a model that prioritizes high sensitivity, even at the cost of lower specificity, to ensure no events are missed
Develop a model that balances sensitivity and specificity based on a cost-benefit analysis of false positives vs. false negatives
Develop a model that incorporates contextual information (e.g., time of night, sleep stage) to improve event classification accuracy
review: Controls model sensitivity vs. model specificity. Weakness: The options do not consider the computational cost and scalability of different model architectures.

name: Facility Expansion Timing
consequences: The timing of facility expansion impacts the project's capacity to enroll participants and collect data. Delaying expansion until the pilot phase is complete minimizes financial risk but may slow down overall progress. Expanding prematurely could strain resources and compromise data quality if operational challenges are not adequately addressed.
options: Initiate facility expansion immediately after the pilot cohort to maximize enrollment capacity from year two
Defer facility expansion until month 24, contingent on successful completion of key milestones and external advisory board approval
Abandon facility expansion entirely, focusing on optimizing data collection and analysis within the existing 8-suite capacity
review: Controls enrollment capacity vs. financial risk. Weakness: The options do not account for potential delays in equipment procurement or construction timelines.

name: Control Group Matching Criteria
consequences: The criteria used to match control participants with parasomnia participants influence the validity of the study's comparative analyses. Strict matching on demographic variables may reduce confounding factors but limit the availability of suitable controls. Relaxing matching criteria may increase the sample size but introduce more variability and reduce the statistical power of the study.
options: Match controls strictly on age, sex, and BMI to minimize confounding variables
Match controls loosely on age and sex, prioritizing recruitment speed and sample size
Recruit a convenience sample of healthy sleepers from the local community without formal matching criteria
review: Controls statistical power vs. confounding variables. Weakness: The options fail to address the potential for unmeasured confounders, such as sleep hygiene practices.

name: PSG Montage Density
consequences: Increasing PSG montage density (number of electrodes and sensors) improves signal resolution and the ability to detect subtle physiological changes. However, it also increases participant burden, technician workload for application and removal, and data complexity for annotation. A denser montage may also disrupt sleep and reduce the ecological validity of the data.
options: Employ a fixed, comprehensive PSG montage on all enhanced nights to maximize data capture
Use a minimalist PSG montage focused on core sleep architecture and parasomnia indicators to reduce burden
Adapt the PSG montage based on participant characteristics and suspected parasomnia subtypes to target specific signals
review: Controls data richness vs. participant comfort. Weakness: The options do not consider the impact of montage density on inter-rater reliability of sleep scoring.

name: Event Definition Stringency
consequences: The stringency of event definitions directly impacts the number of events identified and the workload for manual review. More stringent definitions reduce false positives but may miss genuine parasomnia episodes. Looser definitions increase sensitivity but require more manual filtering and adjudication, potentially overwhelming the annotation team.
options: Adopt highly specific, conservative event definitions to minimize false positives and annotation burden
Use broad, inclusive event definitions to maximize sensitivity and capture all potential parasomnia episodes
Implement adaptive event definitions that adjust stringency based on participant characteristics and sensor data quality
review: Controls sensitivity vs. specificity of event detection. Weakness: The options fail to address the potential for bias introduced by subjective event definitions.

name: Control Group Stratification
consequences: The method of stratifying the control group affects the validity of comparisons and the generalizability of findings. A poorly matched control group can lead to spurious results and inaccurate benchmarking of the event-triage tools. Overly strict matching criteria may limit recruitment and introduce selection bias.
options: Match controls solely on age and sex to maximize recruitment and statistical power
Match controls on a broad range of demographic and sleep-related variables to minimize confounding factors
Recruit a convenience sample of healthy sleepers without explicit matching to assess baseline sensor activity
review: Controls internal validity vs. external validity. Weakness: The options do not consider the ethical implications of recruiting healthy controls for sleep studies.

name: Data Anonymization Depth
consequences: The depth of data anonymization impacts participant privacy and the potential for re-identification. More aggressive anonymization reduces privacy risks but may limit the utility of the data for certain analyses. Insufficient anonymization exposes participants to potential privacy breaches and legal liabilities.
options: Apply minimal anonymization, retaining quasi-identifiers for detailed subgroup analysis within the research team
Employ a comprehensive anonymization protocol, removing all quasi-identifiers and limiting data granularity for all users
Use a tiered anonymization approach, granting differential data access based on user roles and data security protocols
review: Controls data utility vs. participant privacy. Weakness: The options do not address the challenges of anonymizing video data effectively.

name: Facility Access Restrictions
consequences: The stringency of facility access restrictions affects participant safety, data security, and the overall research environment. Overly restrictive access can create an artificial and stressful environment, potentially altering sleep behavior. Insufficient restrictions can compromise participant safety and data integrity.
options: Implement open access during daytime hours to promote a naturalistic environment
Restrict access to authorized personnel only at all times to maximize security and minimize disruptions
Establish tiered access levels based on role and training, balancing security with operational needs
review: Controls participant comfort vs. data security. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact of access restrictions on staff morale and workflow.

name: Episode Trigger Thresholds
consequences: The threshold for triggering 'escalation nights' with full PSG impacts resource allocation and data capture. Low thresholds increase the likelihood of capturing rare events but consume more technician time and PSG resources. High thresholds reduce resource strain but may miss critical parasomnia episodes.
options: Set a low threshold for escalation nights to maximize the capture of potential parasomnia events
Set a high threshold for escalation nights to conserve resources and focus on clearly defined events
Dynamically adjust the escalation threshold based on individual participant's event history and risk profile
review: Controls resource utilization vs. event capture rate. Weakness: The options do not account for the potential for habituation effects on event frequency.

name: Night Technician Responsibilities
consequences: Altering the night technician's responsibilities directly impacts staffing costs and data annotation throughput. More responsibilities per technician could reduce personnel expenses but might compromise data quality or increase the risk of errors. Fewer responsibilities could improve data quality but increase staffing needs.
options: Delegate real-time event flagging and preliminary scoring to the night technician
Limit the night technician to safety monitoring and basic equipment checks only
Assign the night technician responsibility for participant interaction and comfort in addition to monitoring
review: Controls cost vs. data quality. Weakness: The options do not consider the technician's skill level or training needs.

name: Residential Stay Duration
consequences: The length of residential stays affects participant recruitment, data yield, and overall project timeline. Shorter stays reduce costs and allow for faster participant turnover, but may decrease the likelihood of capturing parasomnia events. Longer stays increase the chance of capturing events but can strain resources and potentially increase participant burden.
options: Implement a fixed 4-week residential stay for all participants
Tailor stay duration to individual event frequency, extending stays until a target number of events are captured
Offer tiered stay durations with incentives for longer participation
review: Controls data yield vs. participant burden. Weakness: The options fail to address the impact of seasonal variations on sleep patterns.

name: Environmental Sensor Integration
consequences: The extent of environmental sensor integration influences the richness of contextual data and the complexity of data analysis. More sensors provide a more detailed picture of the sleep environment but increase data storage and processing demands. Fewer sensors simplify data management but may miss relevant contextual factors influencing parasomnia events.
options: Integrate comprehensive environmental monitoring including light, temperature, humidity, and air quality
Focus solely on ambient sound and infrared motion detection
Exclude environmental sensors entirely, relying on participant self-report for contextual information
review: Controls data richness vs. analytical complexity. Weakness: The options do not account for sensor calibration and maintenance costs.

name: Video Data Handling
consequences: The approach to video data handling impacts privacy, data security, and the potential for detailed behavioral analysis. Restricting video access protects participant privacy but limits the ability to identify subtle behavioral cues. Broad video access enables comprehensive analysis but raises ethical and legal concerns.
options: Restrict video access to the PI and designated scoring personnel only
Implement automated masking of identifiable features in video data
Allow participants to opt-in to broader video data sharing for research purposes
review: Controls privacy vs. data granularity. Weakness: The options do not consider the computational resources needed for video processing.

name: Facility Safety Protocols
consequences: The stringency of facility safety protocols affects participant safety, operational costs, and the perceived clinical atmosphere. More stringent protocols minimize risk but can increase costs and create a less naturalistic environment. Less stringent protocols reduce costs but may compromise participant safety.
options: Implement continuous video monitoring with immediate technician response to any unusual activity
Rely on scheduled checks and participant self-reporting of safety concerns
Integrate wearable physiological monitors with automated alerts for falls or other emergencies
review: Controls participant safety vs. environmental naturalism. Weakness: The options do not address the psychological impact of safety measures on participants.

name: Control Group Selection
consequences: The method of control group selection influences the validity of the study's findings and the generalizability of the event-triage model. Closely matched controls provide a strong baseline for comparison but may be difficult to recruit. Less stringently matched controls are easier to recruit but may introduce confounding variables.
options: Recruit controls from the general population with no sleep complaints
Match controls based on age, sex, and BMI only
Recruit controls with other sleep disorders but no parasomnias
review: Controls internal validity vs. recruitment feasibility. Weakness: The options fail to consider the impact of medication use on control group data.
